reviews.domain: Product/Vision layer (README.md, vision.md, product-constraints.md, uvp.md, tech-stack.md, project-structure.md, glossary.md) cross-validated against decisions/, reference/, roadmap/, architecture/, and memory.
crossDocConflicts: KNOWN ISSUE #1 (CONFIRMED + extended into product layer): Loop & Subworkflow forward-reference gap. reference/shared-core/node-types.md lists `loop` and `subworkflow` in the engine enum and renders `LoopNode` as a canvas component; glossary.md counts `LoopNode` among 'the nine canvas node types' and defines it as executable ('repeats a sub-graph until a condition is met'); roadmap phase-3-desktop.md 3.E builds LoopNode on the canvas and 3.M serializes the canvas to YAML. BUT roadmap phase-1-engine-and-llm.md 1.P scopes node handlers only for condition/fan_out/fan_in/transform/input/output (+agent, +human_in_the_loop) — NO loop or subworkflow handler — and workflow-yaml-spec.md v1.0 marks loop/subworkflow YAML `type` as '(reserved)'. Net effect a reader hits weeks from now: a desktop user can drag a LoopNode and save, but there is no YAML `type` to serialize it to and no Phase-1 engine handler to run it. The product layer (glossary) presents Loop as a fully-shipped Phase-1 feature; the engine/schema layer reserves it. DECIDE & STATE ONCE: reserve (forward-compat, not executable/authorable in v1.0 — then glossary must say so and the canvas must not offer LoopNode in Phase 1) vs build (then Phase 0 NodeSchema + Phase 1 1.P + YAML v1.0 must scope loop, and node-types.md must un-reserve the YAML `type`).

KNOWN ISSUE #2 (CONFIRMED): node-type vocabulary/count drift is reconciled by reference/shared-core/node-types.md but the product layer does not fully agree. glossary.md asserts a flat 'nine canvas node types' and defines each as if authorable; workflow-yaml-spec.md exposes 8 YAML `type`s (no loop, no tool-as-node, no subworkflow); node-types.md engine enum is 11 (`agent|condition|tool|transform|input|output|loop|fan_out|fan_in|human_in_the_loop|subworkflow`); architecture/shared-core-engine.md repeats 'the nine canvas node types: Agent, Condition, FanOut, Aggregator, Loop, HumanGate, Input, Output, Tool'. The parallel→fan_out/fan_in and human_gate→human_in_the_loop mappings live only in node-types.md; the product-layer glossary uses HumanGateNode/FanOutNode names without ever signposting that the YAML author types `human_gate`/`parallel`/`merge`. node-types.md should be cited as the single reconciling source from glossary.md (it currently links but does not flag the name divergence).

KNOWN ISSUE #3 (CONFIRMED, not in my scope to fix but cross-checked): roadmap/phases/phase-3-desktop.md uses `3.M` as a workstream id (YAML import/export + starter templates) AND `3.M-shell`, `3.M-manage`, `3.M-canvas`, `3.M-theater`, `3.M-history` as in-phase milestone ids. The `3.M-*` milestone prefix collides with workstream `3.M`, and the global spine uses M3/M4 — ambiguous. Renumber the in-phase milestone prefix (e.g. `3.MS-shell` or `P3.M1`).

KNOWN ISSUE #4 (CONFIRMED): gate-paused CLI exit code is still unpinned. reference/cli/commands.md exit-code table lists 0/1/2 and a note 'A dedicated non-zero code for run paused at a human gate is planned ... the exact value is not yet locked.' roadmap/phase-2-cli.md 2.A says 'lock the value here, since the reference leaves it open, and document it in commands.md' but never states the integer; 2.F and exit criteria reference 'the gate-paused code' abstractly. The two docs agree it is unpinned — they need to agree on a concrete integer (e.g. `3`).

ADAPTER COUNT drift between product layer and engine layer: project-structure.md names FOUR adapters in packages/llm ('AnthropicAdapter, OpenAIAdapter, GeminiAdapter, DeepSeekAdapter'), but ADR-0011, tech-stack.md, architecture/multi-llm-providers.md, and roadmap (M1/M2, phase-1 1.G–1.J) consistently say THREE adapters because DeepSeek shares the OpenAI-compatible adapter via a custom baseURL. A reader reconciling 'DeepSeekAdapter' in project-structure.md against '3 adapters / DeepSeek reuses the OpenAI adapter' everywhere else will see a contradiction.

TRIGGER-PHASING wording mismatch between product layer and reference. product-constraints.md states webhook/schedule triggers are 'Phase 2' and 'The file_change trigger ships in Phase 1; webhook and schedule triggers require a cloud listener and are therefore Phase 2.' glossary.md similarly marks webhook/schedule as '(Phase 2)'. BUT workflow-yaml-spec.md says webhook/schedule are 'declarable in the file' and 'In Phase 1 (local-first) they are honored when the workflow is invoked manually or by a watcher the user runs; cloud-hosted automatic firing is a Phase 2 capability.' These are reconcilable but the product-layer flat 'Phase 2' claim reads as 'not present at all in Phase 1,' whereas the spec says the trigger type is authorable/honored-on-manual-invoke in Phase 1 and only automatic firing is Phase 2. Align the nuance (declarable+manual in P1 vs auto-fire in P2) across product-constraints.md and glossary.md.

MANUAL-vs-FILE_CHANGE 'only Phase 1 trigger' phrasing is inconsistent. glossary.md (manual entry) says manual is 'The only fully-supported Phase 1 trigger alongside file_change'; workflow-yaml-spec.md calls manual 'the only Phase-1 trigger that fires automatically from a surface'; product-constraints.md note says 'The file_change trigger ships in Phase 1.' Three slightly different framings of which triggers are Phase 1. They do not contradict outright but a reader cannot tell from the product layer alone whether file_change is first-class Phase 1 or 'the engine's' trigger exercised only by later surfaces (phase-2-cli.md 2.O-area calls file_change 'the engine's file_change ... exercised by later surfaces').

findings.issue: LoopNode is presented as a fully-shipped, executable Phase-1 canvas node ('LoopNode — repeats a sub-graph until a condition is met', counted in 'The nine canvas node types'), but reference/shared-core/node-types.md marks the loop YAML `type` as '(reserved)', workflow-yaml-spec.md v1.0 does not expose a loop type, and roadmap phase-1 1.P scopes no loop engine handler. A reader weeks from now will believe loops are authorable/runnable in Phase 1 when the schema and engine reserve them. This is the product-layer face of KNOWN ISSUE #1.
findings.proposedFix: Once the reserve-vs-build decision is made (recommend RESERVE for v1.0), make the glossary agree: either (a) move LoopNode/Subworkflow into a 'Reserved (forward-compat, not executable in Phase 1 / not authorable in YAML v1.0)' sub-bullet and stop counting Loop in the shippable canvas set, or (b) if building, ensure Phase 0 NodeSchema + Phase 1 1.P + YAML v1.0 add `loop` and update node-types.md to un-reserve it. The glossary must not describe a node as executable that the v1.0 schema reserves.

findings.issue: The 'Node Types (canvas catalog)' section lists nine canvas component names (AgentNode, FanOutNode, AggregatorNode, HumanGateNode, etc.) but never signposts that these are NOT the values an author writes in YAML — the author writes `agent`, `parallel`, `merge`, `human_gate`. node-types.md is the reconciling source but the glossary links to it only for 'full props,' not for the name divergence. A newcomer reading the glossary first (per README reading order) will assume the canvas names are the YAML types.
findings.proposedFix: Add a one-line note under the canvas-catalog heading: 'These are the canvas component names; the value authored in YAML differs (e.g. parallel→FanOut/Aggregator, human_gate→HumanGateNode). See node-types.md for the canonical canvas↔YAML↔engine reconciliation table.' This makes node-types.md the explicit single source the product layer defers to (KNOWN ISSUE #2).

findings.issue: packages/llm row names four adapters including 'DeepSeekAdapter', contradicting ADR-0011 / tech-stack.md / multi-llm-providers.md / roadmap (M1, M2, phase-1 1.G–1.J) which all state there are THREE adapters because OpenAI and DeepSeek share one OpenAI-compatible adapter (DeepSeek via custom baseURL). The '3 adapters' count is asserted in the milestone gates, so the stray 'DeepSeekAdapter' in project-structure.md is a real cross-doc contradiction.
findings.proposedFix: Change the project-structure.md packages/llm line to '(AnthropicAdapter, GeminiAdapter, and a shared OpenAICompatibleAdapter serving both OpenAI and DeepSeek)' so the adapter count matches the '3 adapters' used in tech-stack.md, ADR-0011, and the roadmap milestones.

findings.issue: The Out-of-Scope table + note flatly classify webhook/schedule triggers as 'Phase 2', which reads as 'not present in Phase 1 at all'. workflow-yaml-spec.md says the trigger types are declarable and honored on manual/watcher invocation in Phase 1, with only cloud-hosted automatic firing deferred to Phase 2. The product-layer simplification can mislead a reader into thinking a webhook-typed workflow is rejected by the Phase 1 schema.
findings.proposedFix: Reword the constraint to match the spec's nuance: 'webhook/schedule triggers are declarable in Phase 1 (and honored when the workflow is invoked manually/by a user-run watcher); automatic cloud-hosted firing is Phase 2.' Keep one canonical phrasing and reuse it in glossary.md.

findings.issue: Three docs give three different framings of which triggers are 'Phase 1': glossary ('manual is the only fully-supported Phase 1 trigger alongside file_change'), workflow-yaml-spec ('manual is the only Phase-1 trigger that fires automatically from a surface'), product-constraints ('file_change ships in Phase 1'). The phrase 'fully-supported … alongside file_change' vs 'fires automatically from a surface' vs phase-2-cli.md treating file_change as 'the engine's trigger exercised by later surfaces' leaves a reader unsure whether file_change is a first-class Phase-1 surface trigger or an engine capability not wired until desktop (phase 3).
findings.proposedFix: Adopt one canonical sentence across glossary.md, workflow-yaml-spec.md, and product-constraints.md, e.g.: 'Phase 1 triggers: manual (all surfaces) and file_change (wired in the desktop app, Phase 3). webhook/schedule are declarable but auto-fire only in Phase 2.' This removes the 'fully-supported' vs 'fires automatically' ambiguity.

findings.issue: Gate-paused exit code is left unpinned ('the exact value is not yet locked'), and roadmap phase-2-cli.md 2.A instructs to 'lock the value here ... and document it in commands.md' but also never names the integer. Both docs therefore agree only that it is open. For a contract doc marked 'CI relies on deterministic exit codes', an unpinned code is a hole a CI author hits directly (KNOWN ISSUE #4).
findings.proposedFix: Pin a concrete integer (recommend `3` — 0/1/2 are taken) in the commands.md exit-code table, replace the 'not yet locked' note with the value, and make phase-2-cli.md 2.A/2.F reference exit code 3 explicitly instead of 'the gate-paused code'.

findings.issue: In-phase milestone ids use the prefix '3.M-' (3.M-shell, 3.M-manage, 3.M-canvas, 3.M-theater, 3.M-history) while '3.M' is simultaneously a workstream id (Workflow YAML import/export + starter templates), and the global spine uses M3/M4. The collision makes '3.M' ambiguous between a workstream and a milestone-namespace (KNOWN ISSUE #3).
findings.proposedFix: Renumber the in-phase milestone prefix to avoid the workstream id, e.g. 'P3.M1 … P3.M5' or 'ms-shell/ms-canvas', and update the Milestones table + any references. (Outside my doc scope but confirmed; flagging for the roadmap owner.)

findings.issue: vision.md 'Killer Features (Phase 1)' lists 'Human gate with timeout and escalation' and 'Multi-model fallback chains' as Phase-1 features, and the canvas execution theater with 'parallel branches stream simultaneously' — all consistent. However it does NOT list or hint at any reserved/forward-looking nodes, so a reader cross-referencing vision.md against node-types.md (which shows Loop/Subworkflow) has no product-layer anchor explaining why those exist but are reserved. Minor: vision is internally consistent but silent on the reserve decision.
findings.proposedFix: Optional: after the reserve-vs-build decision, add a single clause in vision.md or product-constraints.md noting that loop/subworkflow node types are reserved for a post-v1.0 schema increment, so the engine-enum entries in node-types.md have a product-layer home and are not read as an undocumented Phase-1 feature.

reviews.verdict: NOT READY TO MERGE AS-IS — the product/vision layer is high-quality, internally consistent on the big rocks (surfaces, phasing, local-first, desktop-not-an-IDE, internal @relavium/llm with no Vercel/LangChain, Tauri/monorepo layout, tagline, Hono-on-Bun, four-providers/three-adapters intent), and the no-front-matter/H1+bold-metadata/relative-link house style holds throughout. All four known issues are CONFIRMED and one (Loop/Subworkflow) leaks directly into the product layer via glossary.md, which presents LoopNode as a shipped executable Phase-1 node while the schema (workflow-yaml-spec v1.0) and engine (phase-1 1.P handlers) reserve it. Two new product-layer drifts found: (a) project-structure.md names a 'DeepSeekAdapter' contradicting the '3 adapters / DeepSeek shares the OpenAI adapter' stated everywhere else, and (b) webhook/schedule trigger phasing is flattened to 'Phase 2' in the product layer vs the spec's 'declarable-in-P1, auto-fire-in-P2' nuance. Recommend: (1) make the reserve-vs-build call for loop/subworkflow and align glossary.md + node-types.md + workflow-yaml-spec.md + phase-0/1 to it; (2) fix the DeepSeekAdapter count in project-structure.md; (3) pin the gate-paused exit code to a concrete integer in commands.md; (4) renumber phase-3 '3.M-*' milestones; (5) unify trigger-phasing wording across glossary.md/product-constraints.md/workflow-yaml-spec.md. None are blocking for a docs-only commit, but all should be resolved before the schema/engine work in Phase 0–1 starts, since they touch the frozen contracts those phases build against.

reviews.domain: architecture (docs/architecture/*.md) cross-validated against reference/ specs and decisions/ ADRs
reviews.verdict: NOT READY TO COMMIT. The 8 architecture docs are individually clean and structurally consistent with the canonical sources on the big-ticket items (one shared engine, the @relavium/llm internal seam + no-Vercel stance, local-first/Phase-2 marking, keychain trust boundary, cost:updated payload, sequenceNumber). But cross-validation surfaces several genuine contradictions that will mislead a reader weeks from now: (1) run-event names in execution-model.md and state-management.md use node:error / run:error / human_gate:pending, which are NOT in the canonical RunEvent union (node:failed / run:failed / human_gate:paused/:resumed) and are in fact the deprecated dotted-era verbs the SSE schema explicitly retired; (2) the LLM stream-chunk vocabulary in multi-llm-providers.md (text/tool_call/usage/finish) contradicts the canonical seam (text_delta/tool_call_start/tool_call_delta/tool_call_end/stop/error) — note ADR-0011 carries the same stale chunk names, so the canonical seam doc is the lone source of truth and two docs drift from it; (3) the engine entry-point method name drifts (WorkflowEngine.start(workflowId, input) in the architecture docs vs engine.run()/engine.resume() in sse-event-schema.md and cloud-phase-2.md); (4) the known Loop/Subworkflow forward-reference gap reaches the architecture layer — shared-core-engine.md presents Loop as one of "the nine canvas node types" the DAG is built from with no note that loop/subworkflow are reserved/non-executable in the Phase-1 engine (Phase 1 workstream 1.P scopes only condition/fan-out/fan-in/transform/input/output); (5) desktop-architecture.md forward-references a "tracked top risk ... see overview.md" that overview.md does not contain. Fix the event-name and chunk-name drift before commit (high severity — they contradict frozen contracts that Phase 0/1 pin by test); the rest are medium/low.

findings.issue: Uses run-event names that contradict the canonical RunEvent union in reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md. Line 81-82 lists the event set as 'node:completed / node:error / human_gate:pending / cost:updated / run:completed / run:error'; line 100 'the engine emits human_gate:pending'; line 128 'emits run:completed (or run:error if the run failed)'. The canonical union has NO node:error, run:error, or human_gate:pending — it defines node:failed, run:failed, human_gate:paused, human_gate:resumed. Worse, node.error/run.complete/human_gate.pending are exactly the deprecated dotted-era names the SSE schema's 'Legacy event-name note' says new code must NOT use. Phase-0 0.E and Phase-1 1.N pin the colon-namespaced names by type-level AND runtime test, so this doc would fail its own contract.
findings.proposedFix: Replace node:error -> node:failed, run:error -> run:failed, human_gate:pending -> human_gate:paused throughout execution-model.md (lines 19-20 state-diagram labels, 81-82 the event list, 100, 128). For the gate, reflect the suspend/resume pair: emit human_gate:paused on suspend and human_gate:resumed on decision (matching sse-event-schema.md lines 64-65 and Phase-1 1.Q).

findings.issue: Same event-name drift as execution-model.md. Line 132-134 routes 'run:error' and 'human_gate:pending' into runStore and says 'A human_gate:pending event raises the root-level HumanGateOverlay.' These names are not in the canonical RunEvent union (run:failed / human_gate:paused). Phase-3 3.F correctly uses human_gate:paused/:resumed and node:failed, so this doc also contradicts the roadmap it supports.
findings.proposedFix: Change run:error -> run:failed and human_gate:pending -> human_gate:paused on lines 132-134; the overlay is raised by human_gate:paused and cleared/continued by human_gate:resumed.

findings.issue: The normalized stream-chunk vocabulary contradicts the canonical LLMProvider seam. Lines 83-84 and 101 describe the chunk union as 'text (delta) / tool_call ({id,name,argsDelta}) / usage ({input,output}) / finish ({reason})'. The canonical seam (reference/shared-core/llm-provider-seam.md lines 80-87) defines StreamChunk as text_delta / tool_call_start / tool_call_delta (argsJsonDelta) / tool_call_end / stop (carries stopReason+usage) / error. There is no separate 'usage' or 'finish' chunk and no single 'tool_call' chunk in the canonical type. Phase-1 1.A says the seam is frozen 'exactly as llm-provider-seam.md specifies', so the architecture doc is the drifted one. (Note: ADR-0011 line 23 carries the same stale text/tool_call/usage/finish names, so it should be reconciled in the same pass even though it is outside the architecture folder.)
findings.proposedFix: Update multi-llm-providers.md to the canonical chunk union: 'text_delta / tool_call_start / tool_call_delta (argsJsonDelta) / tool_call_end / stop (carries stopReason + usage) / error', and drop the description of separate 'usage' and 'finish' chunks (usage rides on the final stop chunk). Optionally add a one-line cite that the canonical shape lives in llm-provider-seam.md. Flag ADR-0011 line 23 for the same correction.

findings.issue: Forward-reference gap on Loop (and Subworkflow). Line 85 states the DAG 'is built from ... the nine canvas node types: Agent, Condition, FanOut, Aggregator, Loop, HumanGate, Input, Output, Tool', and the run-loop section (88-101) presents the dispatch table as if every listed type executes. But node-types.md marks Loop's YAML type as (reserved) and Subworkflow as (reserved) in both canvas and YAML, and Phase-1 workstream 1.P scopes engine handlers to only condition/fan-out/fan-in/transform/input/output (no loop, no subworkflow). A reader concludes Loop is executable in the first engine increment when it is not. This is the cross-tree manifestation of known issue #1 in the architecture layer.
findings.proposedFix: In shared-core-engine.md, when listing the node types and in the run-loop dispatch table, add a one-line note that loop and subworkflow are reserved/forward-compat engine enum members not executed by the Phase-1 engine increment (handlers land in a later increment), citing node-types.md as the canonical reconciliation and Phase-1 1.P scope. Keep the decision (reserve vs build) consistent with node-types.md's '(reserved)' marking.

findings.issue: Dangling forward-reference. Lines 63-64 say cross-platform WebView rendering 'is a tracked top risk (see overview.md) with a Playwright-based per-platform test suite as the mitigation.' overview.md contains no risk section and no mention of cross-platform canvas rendering, WebView, or Playwright (grep confirms zero matches). The risk is actually documented in roadmap/phases/phase-3-desktop.md (Risks & mitigations + 3.L). The reader following the link finds nothing.
findings.proposedFix: Point the citation at where the risk actually lives — roadmap/phases/phase-3-desktop.md (Risks & mitigations / the 3.L Playwright per-platform e2e gate) — or add a short 'Risks' subsection to overview.md and keep the link. Do not leave the claim pointing at overview.md, which does not substantiate it.

findings.issue: Engine entry-point method name drifts across the doc set. execution-model.md line 43 and shared-core-engine.md line 56 / overview.md line 152 use 'WorkflowEngine.start(workflowId, input)'. The canonical contract docs use a different name: sse-event-schema.md line 116 'engine.run(workflow, inputs)' and line 134 'engine.resume(runId, gateId, decision)', and cloud-phase-2.md line 70 'Surfaces call engine.run()'. Phase-1 1.N lists 'WorkflowEngine.start / resume / cancel'. A reader cannot tell whether the method is start() or run(), and whether the arg is a workflowId or a workflow object. The architecture docs are internally consistent (start) but disagree with the reference contract and one architecture doc (cloud-phase-2).
findings.proposedFix: Pick one canonical signature and use it everywhere. Recommend aligning to the engine's own API doc (shared-core-engine.md 'What the engine exports' = WorkflowEngine.start/resume/cancel) and updating sse-event-schema.md and cloud-phase-2.md to engine.start(...)/engine.resume(...); or, if run() is preferred, update the three architecture docs. Whichever wins, fix cloud-phase-2.md line 70 so it does not disagree with its sibling architecture docs.

findings.issue: Human-gate timeout and decision vocabulary paraphrases drift from the canonical fields. Line 20 ('on_timeout=fail') and line 110 ('an on_timeout policy (fail, or take a default branch)') mix the engine-config field name on_timeout with a value description ('take a default branch') that matches neither the engine enum (node-types.md: on_timeout = fail/continue_default/cancel) nor the authored YAML field (workflow-yaml-spec.md: timeout_action = reject/approve/escalate). Line 99 describes the gate as the human 'approves, rejects, or edits', whereas the canonical GateDecision verbs are approved/rejected/input_provided (sse-event-schema.md).
findings.proposedFix: In execution-model.md, use the engine-config name with its real enum (on_timeout: fail / continue_default / cancel) or explicitly say it is paraphrasing the authored timeout_action; and change 'approves, rejects, or edits' to 'approves, rejects, or provides input (approved / rejected / input_provided)' to match GateDecision. Keep it as a cite to sse-event-schema.md / node-types.md rather than restating values that can drift.

findings.issue: Aggregator/merge strategy names are conceptual paraphrases that conflate two distinct canonical config fields. execution-model.md line 68 ('all-required, first-wins, quorum-of-N, best-of') and shared-core-engine.md line 97 ('all-required / first-wins / quorum') blend the join policy and the merge strategy. node-types.md fan_in_config separates join_strategy (wait_all / wait_first / wait_n) from merge_strategy (merge_keys / collect_array / first_wins), and 'best-of' is the AggregatorNode merge strategy best_of_n. A reader mapping these to config will not find 'quorum' or 'best-of' as a single strategy.
findings.proposedFix: Either keep these clearly as conceptual examples ('strategies such as ...') AND add a cite to node-types.md fan_in_config for the actual join_strategy/merge_strategy field names, or replace with the canonical terms (wait_all/wait_first/wait_n for the join; best_of_n as a merge strategy). Prefer citing the canonical home rather than restating.

findings.issue: Minor model-id example inconsistency between canonical-id examples. multi-llm-providers.md and the YAML/spec/node-type docs consistently use claude-sonnet-4-6, but the canonical seam doc (llm-provider-seam.md line 34) uses the comment example model: 'claude-sonnet-4'. Not a contradiction in the architecture folder itself, but a reader cross-referencing the seam sees a different canonical-id example.
findings.proposedFix: Align the example canonical id in llm-provider-seam.md line 34 to claude-sonnet-4-6 to match every other doc (this edit is in reference/, surfaced here because the architecture doc is the consistent side).

crossDocConflicts: Run-event verbs: execution-model.md + state-management.md use node:error / run:error / human_gate:pending, which contradict the canonical RunEvent union (node:failed / run:failed / human_gate:paused + human_gate:resumed) in reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md and are the deprecated dotted-era verbs that doc explicitly retired. Phase-0 0.E and Phase-1 1.N pin the canonical names by test.

LLM stream-chunk names: multi-llm-providers.md (text/tool_call/usage/finish) vs canonical seam llm-provider-seam.md (text_delta/tool_call_start/tool_call_delta/tool_call_end/stop/error). ADR-0011 line 23 carries the same stale chunk names, so the fix must touch the ADR too even though it is outside the architecture folder.

Engine entry-point method: WorkflowEngine.start(workflowId, input) (overview.md, execution-model.md, shared-core-engine.md) vs engine.run(workflow, inputs) + engine.resume(runId, gateId, decision) (sse-event-schema.md, cloud-phase-2.md). One name must win across architecture + reference.

Loop/Subworkflow forward-reference gap (known issue #1) reaches architecture: shared-core-engine.md treats Loop as an executable member of 'the nine canvas node types' the DAG is built from, but node-types.md marks loop/subworkflow YAML as (reserved) and Phase-1 1.P does not scope their engine handlers. The architecture doc needs the same reserved/forward-compat note node-types.md carries.

Dangling cross-reference: desktop-architecture.md cites overview.md for a 'tracked top risk' (cross-platform WebView rendering + Playwright) that overview.md does not contain; the risk actually lives in roadmap/phases/phase-3-desktop.md.

Human-gate field/verb paraphrases in execution-model.md (on_timeout value 'take a default branch'; gate verb 'edits') do not match the canonical on_timeout enum (fail/continue_default/cancel), the authored timeout_action enum (reject/approve/escalate), or the GateDecision verbs (approved/rejected/input_provided).

Aggregator strategy paraphrases (quorum-of-N, best-of, all-required) in execution-model.md/shared-core-engine.md conflate the canonical join_strategy (wait_all/wait_first/wait_n) and merge_strategy (merge_keys/collect_array/first_wins/best_of_n) fields in node-types.md.

reviews.verdict: The decisions/ layer is in strong shape and the 0004-to-0011 supersession is fully coherent end-to-end (bidirectional links, accurate README routing table, no doc treats 0004 as live, no Vercel/LangChain endorsement leaks, all 11 ADR Status/Date values match the index, every relative and anchored link resolves). No ADR contradicts another ADR or the current vision on its core decision. However, six cross-validation issues remain that a reader weeks from now would trip on: one genuine three-way cross-document conflict in the auth story that ADR-0008 asserts a side of, plus several self-consistency/terminology/forward-reference gaps within the layer. None are blocking, but the auth conflict (medium) and the README "four sections" error (medium) should be fixed before git. This is read-only; fixes are proposed, not applied.

findings.issue: Three-way auth contradiction that ADR-0008 participates in. ADR-0008 line 23 states Phase-2 auth is via 'Better Auth' (matching tech-stack.md row 'Auth (portal, Phase 2): Better Auth v1' and the architecture-decisions memory). BUT reference/portal/api-reference.md describes auth as 'OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)' with browser sign-in via 'password / Google / SAML / OIDC' — a materially different model. AND product-constraints.md (which ADR-0008 line 35 cites as the authoritative out-of-scope source) lists 'OAuth' as out of scope, 'Portal uses email + password only at first.' So the three docs describe Better-Auth vs OAuth-device-flow-with-Google/SAML/OIDC vs email+password-only-no-OAuth. ADR-0008 asserts the 'Better Auth' side while pointing at a constraints doc that says no OAuth and a portal reference that is all OAuth.
findings.proposedFix: Reconcile to one auth model with one canonical home. Likely intent: Better Auth v1 is the implementation library (tech-stack), it provides email+password for the Phase-2 MVP (product-constraints 'email+password only at first'), and OAuth/SSO device flow is a later Phase-2 increment. Make product-constraints.md and reference/portal/api-reference.md agree on that staging (api-reference currently presents full OAuth device flow + Google/SAML/OIDC as the auth, with no 'email+password first' note). Keep ADR-0008's 'Better Auth' phrasing but add 'see reference/portal/api-reference.md for the auth surface' so the ADR defers the mechanism to one canonical home instead of three docs each asserting a different one.

findings.issue: Internal factual error in the Format section. Line 15 says each ADR 'opens with an H1 title and bold metadata lines, then four sections:' but only THREE sections are then listed (Context, Decision, Consequences). All 11 ADRs and standards/adr-template.md use exactly three sections (Consequences with Positive/Negative subsections). A reader counting sections against the index will be confused about whether a fourth section is missing.
findings.proposedFix: Change 'then four sections:' to 'then three sections:' on line 15. (The bold metadata lines — Status / Date / Related — are listed separately as 'metadata lines', so they are not the missing fourth section.)

findings.issue: Terminology/forward-reference gap: 'segment detection.' ADR-0003 line 27 attributes to Relavium 'its own execution plan (topological sort + segment detection)' as if 'segment detection' is an established part of the engine design. The one-canonical-home engine docs (architecture/shared-core-engine.md, architecture/execution-model.md) describe the plan as 'RunPlan builder (topological sort)' / 'static topological pre-plan plus a dispatch table' and a 'hybrid' model — the term 'segment detection' appears NOWHERE outside ADR-0003. A reader who follows the ADR to learn what 'segment detection' is will find no definition anywhere in the corpus.
findings.proposedFix: Either drop 'segment detection' from ADR-0003 (leaving 'its own execution plan (topological sort)'), or align it with the canonical engine vocabulary. The canonical engine doc uses 'RunPlan builder (topological sort)'; if a segment/parallel-region concept is intended, define it once in architecture/shared-core-engine.md and let ADR-0003 use the same word. Decision-level fix: make ADR-0003's plan vocabulary defer to the engine doc rather than introduce a unique term.

findings.issue: Decisions-layer surfacing of known-issue #1 (loop/subworkflow forward-reference gap). ADR-0003 line 9 lists the engine's executed node set inline as 'agent calls, conditionals, loops, parallel branches, human gates, sub-workflows' with loops and sub-workflows presented on equal footing with agent/condition, no 'reserved / not in first increment' qualifier. The canonical reconciliation table (reference/shared-core/node-types.md) marks loop YAML 'type' as '(reserved)' and subworkflow as '(reserved)' in both canvas and YAML — i.e. not authorable/executable in the first increment. A reader takes ADR-0003 as committing the first engine build to loop+subworkflow execution, which Phase-0 schema and Phase-1 handlers do not scope.
findings.proposedFix: Add a half-clause to ADR-0003 line 9 marking loops and sub-workflows as forward-compat/reserved, e.g. '...human gates, and (reserved for a later increment) loops and sub-workflows — the catalog and which types are reserved live in reference/shared-core/node-types.md.' This keeps the ADR conceptually complete while not over-committing the first increment, and stays consistent with the reserve-vs-build resolution chosen for known-issue #1.

findings.issue: Self-inconsistency with the layer's own stated rule on restating pinned versions. The decisions/README.md (line 21) says 'Pinned tool and library versions are not restated in ADRs; they live in tech-stack.md' and standards/adr-template.md repeats 'Do not restate pinned version numbers.' Yet ADR-0005 (and ADR-0008 line 23) hard-code 'PostgreSQL 16 + Redis 7', and ADR-0010 line 21 hard-codes 'ReactFlow v12' — all of which are the canonical property of tech-stack.md (which lists 'PostgreSQL 16 + Redis 7 + BullMQ' and 'Zustand v5'). If a version bumps in tech-stack.md, these ADR copies silently drift. (Note: 'Tauri v2', 'React 19', 'Phase 2' are part of the decision identity / titles and are fine to keep.)
findings.proposedFix: Either (a) soften the restated minor/major numbers to the un-versioned product name in ADR bodies ('PostgreSQL + Redis', 'ReactFlow') and let tech-stack.md own the pin, or (b) relax the README/template rule to explicitly permit naming the major version that is part of the decision's identity while still forbidding patch/minor pins. Pick one and apply consistently so the stated rule and the ADR bodies agree.

findings.issue: Unused citation convention drift. The README (line 32) establishes a citation convention: 'Product ADR-00NN' (this folder) vs 'Cloud ADR-00NN' (relavium-cloud). But that prefixed form is used NOWHERE in the corpus — every ADR and every inbound citation (tech-stack, roadmap, standards, architecture) uses the bare 'ADR-00NN'. A reader who internalizes the README convention will expect 'Product ADR-' prefixes and find none, and will not know whether a bare 'ADR-0008' means the product space. Low risk today since the cloud ADR space does not yet exist, but it is a stated-but-unfollowed rule.
findings.proposedFix: Soften the convention to reflect reality: state that within this repo bare 'ADR-00NN' always means a Product ADR, and the explicit 'Product ADR-' / 'Cloud ADR-' prefixes are only required once the Phase-2 cloud repo exists and cross-repo citation becomes possible. That makes the current bare usage compliant rather than a silent violation of the README's own rule.

crossDocConflicts: AUTH MODEL (3-way): decisions/0008-local-first-phase-1-cloud-phase-2.md + tech-stack.md say Phase-2 auth = 'Better Auth v1'; reference/portal/api-reference.md says auth = 'OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)' with password/Google/SAML/OIDC; product-constraints.md (cited as authoritative by ADR-0008) says 'OAuth' is out of scope and 'Portal uses email + password only at first.' Three documents describe three different auth models; ADR-0008 asserts the Better-Auth side while citing a constraints doc that forbids OAuth and pointing readers toward a portal doc that is entirely OAuth. Needs one reconciled staging (Better Auth implementing email+password first, OAuth/SSO as a later Phase-2 increment) with one canonical home.

ENGINE PLAN VOCABULARY: decisions/0003 uses the term 'segment detection' for part of the execution plan; the canonical engine docs (architecture/shared-core-engine.md, architecture/execution-model.md) use 'RunPlan builder (topological sort)' / 'static topological pre-plan plus dispatch table' and never define 'segment detection'. The ADR names a mechanism the one-canonical-home doc does not.

NODE CATALOG (known-issue #1 in the decisions layer): decisions/0003 lists loops and sub-workflows as executed engine nodes with no reserved qualifier, while the canonical reference/shared-core/node-types.md marks loop and subworkflow YAML 'type' as '(reserved)' — i.e. not authorable/executable in the first increment. The ADR over-commits the first engine build relative to the canonical reconciliation table.

PINNED-VERSION RULE vs PRACTICE: decisions/README.md and standards/adr-template.md both say ADRs must NOT restate pinned versions (they live in tech-stack.md), but ADR-0005/0008 restate 'PostgreSQL 16 + Redis 7' and ADR-0010 restates 'ReactFlow v12', duplicating values whose canonical home is tech-stack.md.

CITATION CONVENTION vs PRACTICE: decisions/README.md defines 'Product ADR-00NN' vs 'Cloud ADR-00NN' citation prefixes, but no file in the corpus uses the prefixed form — all use bare 'ADR-00NN'. Stated convention is unfollowed.

reviews.domain: Reference layer cross-validation (docs/reference/**) + cross-cutting architecture/roadmap reconciliation for Relavium
reviews.verdict: NOT READY for git as-is. The reference layer is high quality and mostly one-canonical-home compliant, but cross-document validation surfaced one HIGH-severity contract contradiction (provider-id enum: 'google' vs 'gemini') that would break the agent schema against the LLMProvider seam, plus several medium issues where the node-type taxonomy, gate-timeout field names, and node-status enums disagree across the canonical specs. The four pre-known roadmap issues are all confirmed and extend into the reference layer (node count 9 vs 11, gate exit code unpinned). Fix the HIGH and the taxonomy/gate-field MEDIUMs (small, localized edits), reconcile node-types.md as the single source per the table below, then ship.

findings.issue: PROVIDER-ID CONTRADICTION (cross-doc, contract-breaking). agent-yaml-spec.md declares the provider enum as `'anthropic' | 'openai' | 'google' | 'deepseek'` (line 20, and the examples at lines 74 & 103 use `provider: google`). But the canonical LLMProvider seam (llm-provider-seam.md line 90) defines `readonly id: 'anthropic' | 'openai' | 'gemini' | 'deepseek'` and architecture/multi-llm-providers.md line 169 uses `provider: gemini`. The agent `provider` string is resolved against the seam's provider id (agent-yaml-spec.md line 78 says the model/provider pair is resolved against the LLMProvider seam), so `google` will never match a provider whose id is `gemini`. A reader/implementer cannot tell whether the wire value is `google` or `gemini`. Note model ids use the `gemini-` prefix (e.g. `gemini-2.5-pro`) which makes `gemini` the more likely intended provider id, but the database `llm_providers.name` example only lists `anthropic`/`openai` so it does not disambiguate.
findings.proposedFix: Pick ONE provider id and use it everywhere. Recommend `google` as the user-facing `provider` string (it is the company/account a user recognizes) AND make the seam id match: change llm-provider-seam.md line 90 to `'anthropic' | 'openai' | 'google' | 'deepseek'` and multi-llm-providers.md line 169 to `provider: google`. Alternatively standardize on `gemini` everywhere (change agent-yaml-spec.md lines 20/74/103 and config/db examples). Either way, add the chosen id to the `llm_providers.name` example list in database-schema.md line 69 so the four canonical providers (anthropic, openai, <google|gemini>, deepseek) are enumerated in one place. The seam id and the YAML `provider` string MUST be the same token.

findings.issue: NODE-TYPE COUNT/TAXONOMY DRIFT (3 vocabularies, 3 counts) — confirmed and extended. node-types.md's reconciliation table enumerates 11 concepts and explicitly states the engine enum is `agent | condition | tool | transform | input | output | loop | fan_out | fan_in | human_in_the_loop | subworkflow` (11). But: (a) node-types.md itself says 'nine custom ReactFlow node components' (line 33) — 9 canvas components; (b) workflow-yaml-spec.md lists 8 authored YAML types (input, agent, human_gate, condition, transform, parallel, merge, output); (c) database-schema.md line 214 constrains `step_executions.node_type` to 'one of the 9 node types' — but a `step_execution` row records the ENGINE node that ran, which is the 11-value enum (fan_out, fan_in, loop, subworkflow, tool can all execute as steps), so '9' is wrong for that column. Phase-0 roadmap models only 8 YAML types and Phase-1 only scopes 7 engine handlers, so loop/subworkflow are forward-references (see separate finding).
findings.proposedFix: Make node-types.md the single reconciling source and state the three counts explicitly and consistently: 8 authored YAML types, 9 canvas components, 11 engine enum values (of which `loop`/`subworkflow` are reserved/forward-compat — see the loop/subworkflow finding). Fix database-schema.md line 214 to say `node_type` is 'one of the engine node-type values (see node-types.md)' rather than 'one of the 9 node types', since step rows persist engine-side types. Keep routes-and-screens.md line 54 and README 'nine canvas node types' as canvas-component count (correct) but have both link to node-types.md as authoritative. Canonical mapping table is provided in crossDocConflicts.

findings.issue: GATE TIMEOUT FIELD-NAME + ENUM DISAGREEMENT across the three canonical specs. workflow-yaml-spec.md uses authored field `timeout_action` with values `reject | approve | escalate` (lines 135, 174, 177). node-types.md engine config uses `on_timeout` with values `fail | continue_default | cancel` (line 102) and separately uses `timeout_seconds` (engine) vs the YAML's `timeout_ms`. architecture/execution-model.md (lines 20, 110) describes the policy as `on_timeout` with values `fail` / 'take a default branch' — i.e. it uses the ENGINE field name in a narrative that should describe author-facing behavior, and its value set ({fail, default-branch}) matches neither the YAML's {reject, approve, escalate} nor cleanly maps to the engine's {fail, continue_default, cancel}. There is no published mapping from authored `reject/approve/escalate` to engine `fail/continue_default/cancel`, so a reader cannot determine what `escalate` compiles to (and sse-event-schema.md line 148 only says timeout maps to `decidedBy: 'timeout_escalation'`).
findings.proposedFix: In node-types.md (the reconciliation home), add the authored→engine timeout mapping explicitly next to the parallel→fan_out/fan_in mapping note, e.g.: authored `timeout_action: reject` → engine `on_timeout: fail` (or a rejecting auto-decision), `timeout_action: approve` → an auto-approve decision, `timeout_action: escalate` → notify-fallback + extend (which has no direct single engine enum and needs definition). Also reconcile `timeout_ms` (authored, ms) ↔ `timeout_seconds` (engine, s) in the same place. Then change execution-model.md lines 20 & 110 to reference the authored `timeout_action` values (or explicitly label it as the engine `on_timeout` policy) so the narrative and the contract agree. The three values must reconcile in exactly one table.

findings.issue: LEGACY/DOTTED EVENT-NAME DRIFT in a current architecture doc. execution-model.md lines 81-82, 100, 128 emit `node:error`, `run:error`, and `human_gate:pending`. The canonical RunEvent union (sse-event-schema.md lines 38-68) uses `node:failed`, `run:failed`, and `human_gate:paused`. sse-event-schema.md line 161 explicitly lists `node.error`/`run.complete`/`human_gate.pending` as LEGACY names superseded by the colon-namespaced union. The colon-namespaced-but-wrong-stem names in execution-model.md (`node:error` not `node:failed`; `human_gate:pending` not `human_gate:paused`) are a partial migration that will mislead a reader into coding to event types that do not exist in the union. Roadmap phase-1 (line 417) and phase-3/4 correctly use `human_gate:paused`/`node:failed`, so execution-model.md is the lone drifter.
findings.proposedFix: In execution-model.md replace `node:error`→`node:failed`, `run:error`→`run:failed` (lines 81-82, 128), and `human_gate:pending`→`human_gate:paused` (lines 81, 100). These must match the canonical union in sse-event-schema.md; the doc already cites that schema as canonical, so the names should mirror it verbatim.

findings.issue: CANONICAL MODEL-ID EXAMPLE INCONSISTENCY. llm-provider-seam.md line 34 gives the canonical id example as `"claude-sonnet-4"`, while every other doc (workflow-yaml-spec, agent-yaml-spec, config-spec, database-schema, node-types, multi-llm-providers) consistently uses `claude-sonnet-4-6`. Since the seam doc is the canonical home for `LlmRequest.model` ('canonical id, e.g. ...'), a reader will reasonably treat `claude-sonnet-4` as the canonical form and `claude-sonnet-4-6` as something else, when they are meant to be the same model. The seam's own `costUsd` is 'keyed on the canonical model id', so an off-by-suffix canonical id would silently miss the pricing-table row.
findings.proposedFix: Change the example on llm-provider-seam.md line 34 from `"claude-sonnet-4"` to `"claude-sonnet-4-6"` to match the model id used in the model_catalog example (database-schema.md line 91) and every YAML spec. Keep one canonical model-id string across all docs.

findings.issue: RUN-STATUS vs STEP-STATUS ENUM MISMATCH around the human-gate pause state. `runs.status` CHECK allows `'pending','running','paused','completed','failed','cancelled'` (line 184) — includes `paused`, and the execution-model state diagram has an `AwaitingGate` state. But `step_executions.status` CHECK allows only `'pending','running','completed','failed','skipped'` (line 219) — no `paused`. When a run suspends at a human_gate, the human_gate step row's status has no value to represent 'suspended/awaiting decision': it cannot be `completed` (no decision yet), `failed`, or `running` (the process may have exited per sse-event-schema.md line 133). A reader implementing the gate-resume query (Common query patterns line 313) cannot tell what status a paused gate step carries.
findings.proposedFix: Either add `'paused'` (or `'awaiting'`) to the `step_executions.status` CHECK constraint to mirror the run-level pause state, or add a one-line note in database-schema.md stating how a suspended human_gate step is represented (e.g. it stays `running` while the run is `paused`, and the gate state lives in run_events/checkpoint). Make the gate-pause representation explicit in exactly one place.

findings.issue: CONFIRMED known issue #3 — milestone id collision. The in-phase milestone ids 3.M-shell / 3.M-manage / 3.M-canvas / 3.M-theater / 3.M-history (lines 476-480) collide with workstream id `3.M` ('Workflow YAML import/export + starter templates', defined at line 434 and in the dep graph line 111). Worse, the dependency cell for `3.M-canvas` lists its deps as `3.E, 3.M` (line 478) — so a milestone whose own id prefix is `3.M-*` depends on workstream `3.M`, which is genuinely confusing/ambiguous to a reader weeks later (is `3.M` the workstream or shorthand for a milestone?).
findings.proposedFix: Renumber the in-phase milestones off the `3.M` prefix to avoid collision with workstream `3.M`. Suggest `MS3.1`-`MS3.5` (or `3.MS-shell`, etc.). Then the `3.M-canvas` dependency cell unambiguously reads `3.E, 3.M` = workstream E + workstream M (import/export), not a milestone depending on a same-prefix token. Apply consistently in the dep graph and the milestone table.

findings.issue: CONFIRMED known issue #4 — gate-paused exit code unpinned, and the two docs that must agree both say 'TBD'. commands.md line 110 says 'A dedicated non-zero code for run paused at a human gate is planned... the exact value is not yet locked.' Exit codes 0/1/2 are pinned (lines 105-108). roadmap phase-2-cli.md repeatedly references 'the gate-paused code' (lines 203, 252-253, 288, 443, 446) and asserts CI tests on it, but never pins the integer either. So a CI author cannot write the assertion, and the two docs that the known-issues list says 'must agree' currently agree only on being unspecified.
findings.proposedFix: Pin a concrete integer for gate-paused in commands.md's exit-code table and have phase-2-cli.md reference that same value. Recommend `75` (EX_TEMPFAIL from sysexits.h — semantically 'temporary failure, try again', which fits 'paused awaiting human, resumable') OR a simple `3` to continue the 0/1/2 sequence. Whichever is chosen, write it in commands.md as code `3` (or `75`) = 'Workflow paused at a human gate (resumable via relavium gate)', and update phase-2-cli.md lines 202-203 / 252-253 to cite the literal value.

findings.issue: CONFIRMED known issue #1 — loop & subworkflow forward-reference gap, now traced across the whole tree. node-types.md commits the engine enum to `loop` and `subworkflow` (and gives LoopNode a full canvas-component spec at lines 59-63, plus per-type engine config blocks `loop_config`/`subworkflow_config` at lines 99/103). Phase-3 roadmap canvas (phase-3-desktop.md lines 243-244, 278) builds `LoopNode` as one of the 'all nine node types' and animates its iteration badge. BUT: Phase-0 schema (phase-0-foundations.md lines 194-195) models only the 8 YAML types (input, agent, human_gate, condition, transform, parallel, merge, output — no loop, no subworkflow), and Phase-1 engine handlers (phase-1-engine-and-llm.md line 448, section 1.P) scope only condition/fan-out/fan-in/transform/input/output (+ agent + human gate) — neither loop nor subworkflow. workflow-yaml-spec.md correctly marks LoopNode/subworkflow as *(reserved)* in node-types.md but does NOT expose any authored `loop`/`subworkflow` YAML type at all. So Phase 3 commits to building a LoopNode component with no Phase-0 schema field and no Phase-1 engine handler behind it.
findings.proposedFix: Decide RESERVE (recommended, matches the *(reserved)* markers already in node-types.md): explicitly state in node-types.md that `loop` and `subworkflow` are forward-compat reserved engine types NOT executable in the first increment (Phase 1), and that the LoopNode canvas component (and any Phase-3 work) is design-only/disabled until the engine handler and a `WorkflowSchema` authored type land in a later phase. Then either (a) drop LoopNode from phase-3-desktop.md's 'all nine node types' build list (making it 8 active canvas components) and move it to a 'reserved/future' note, or (b) keep it but label it non-executable. Add a one-line scope note to phase-0 and phase-1 stating loop/subworkflow are intentionally out of the first increment so the forward reference is explicit rather than a silent gap.

findings.issue: WEB-SEARCH TOOL-ID / KEYCHAIN-ACCOUNT NAMING INCONSISTENCY (minor). built-in-tools.md names the tool `search_web` (line 27) and tauri-plugins.md also says `search_web` (line 39). keychain-and-secrets.md line 50 calls it 'the built-in web-search tool' and stores its key under `account = search-provider`. The keychain entry-naming convention defined two lines above (line 48) is `{providerId}:{keyId}` (e.g. `anthropic:default`), so a bare `search-provider` account does not follow the documented `{providerId}:{keyId}` shape and is not tied to a specific search provider id. Not contradictory, but a reader can't tell whether the search key account is `search-provider`, `search-provider:default`, or keyed by the actual provider (e.g. `brave:default`).
findings.proposedFix: Make the search-key account follow the same `{providerId}:{keyId}` convention, e.g. `account = brave:default` (or `searxng:default`), or explicitly note that `search-provider` is a reserved well-known account id that intentionally deviates from the provider:key form. Reference the tool by its canonical id `search_web` for consistency with built-in-tools.md.

crossDocConflicts:
DEFINITIVE NODE-TYPE TAXONOMY (proposed canonical table; node-types.md owns this). Counts: 8 authored YAML types | 9 canvas components | 11 engine enum values (loop & subworkflow RESERVED/forward-compat, not executable in the first increment). Mapping (Concept -> Canvas component -> authored YAML `type` -> engine enum):
  Entry        -> InputNode      -> input        -> input
  Agent        -> AgentNode      -> agent        -> agent
  Condition    -> ConditionNode  -> condition    -> condition
  Tool         -> ToolNode       -> (via agent tools:) -> tool
  Transform    -> (ConditionNode/inline) -> transform -> transform
  Fan-out      -> FanOutNode     -> parallel     -> fan_out   (the single authored `parallel` expands into TWO engine vertices)
  Fan-in       -> AggregatorNode -> merge        -> fan_in    (the join half of authored `parallel`/`merge`)
  Loop         -> LoopNode       -> (reserved)   -> loop       [RESERVED — no Phase-0 schema field, no Phase-1 handler]
  Human gate   -> HumanGateNode  -> human_gate   -> human_in_the_loop
  Output       -> OutputNode     -> output       -> output
  Subworkflow  -> (reserved)     -> (reserved)   -> subworkflow [RESERVED]
Authoritative authored value = YAML `type`. Canvas-component count = 9 (InputNode, AgentNode, ConditionNode, ToolNode, FanOutNode, AggregatorNode, LoopNode, HumanGateNode, OutputNode). Authored YAML types = 8 (input, agent, human_gate, condition, transform, parallel, merge, output). Engine enum = 11 (agent, condition, tool, transform, input, output, loop, fan_out, fan_in, human_in_the_loop, subworkflow). Every other doc must link here and not re-count: fix database-schema.md line 214 ('one of the 9 node types' -> 'one of the engine node-type values'); keep routes-and-screens.md/README '9 canvas components' but cite this table.

PROVIDER-ID single source: the 4 canonical providers and their ONE id token must be identical in (a) agent-yaml-spec.md `provider` enum, (b) llm-provider-seam.md `LlmProvider.id`, (c) multi-llm-providers.md fallback examples, (d) database-schema.md `llm_providers.name` example. Currently 'google' (agent spec) vs 'gemini' (seam + multi-llm). RESOLVE to one. Recommended canonical set: { anthropic, openai, google, deepseek } with model-id prefix `gemini-*` (note model prefix and provider id differ by design — call that out once in the seam doc). Whatever is chosen, the YAML `provider` string and the seam `id` must be the same literal because agent provider is resolved against the seam.

GATE TIMEOUT reconciliation (node-types.md owns the authored->engine mapping): authored `timeout_action` (reject|approve|escalate) <-> engine `on_timeout` (fail|continue_default|cancel), and authored `timeout_ms` <-> engine `timeout_seconds`. There is currently NO mapping for `escalate` and execution-model.md uses the engine field name with a third value set. Add one mapping table; make execution-model.md's state diagram + narrative use the same field/values.

EVENT-NAME single source = sse-event-schema.md `RunEvent` union (colon-namespaced: run:started, node:started, agent:token, agent:tool_call, agent:tool_result, cost:updated, node:completed, node:failed, human_gate:paused, human_gate:resumed, run:completed, run:failed, run:cancelled). execution-model.md must stop using the wrong stems node:error / run:error / human_gate:pending. Roadmap phases already use the canonical names.

reviews.domain: standards/ layer — binding rules (architectural-principles, documentation-style, adr-template, code-style-typescript, error-handling, testing, code-review, commit-style, security-review, logging-and-observability, README)
reviews.verdict: The standards layer is largely internally consistent and agrees well with tech-stack/decisions/reference: the no-vendor-type-across-the-seam rule, the engine-first build order, the ADR template, the seam type names, and the ≥90% coverage bar all line up with their canonical sources. But there is ONE systemic, high-severity defect: three standards (error-handling, logging-and-observability, security-review) plus testing reference run-event names — node:error, run:error, human_gate:pending — that do NOT exist in the canonical SSE schema (which defines node:failed, run:failed, run:cancelled, human_gate:paused, human_gate:resumed). The standards even instruct contributors to "use the canonical names ... rejected on sight" while themselves using non-canonical ones, so the rule is self-refuting as written. There is also a hollow reference: security-review.md and the README build a whole checklist section around a "run_javascript" built-in tool that the canonical built-in-tools catalog does not contain (it ships run_command instead). Plus a real cross-doc security contradiction (hand-rolled XOR KDF in the keychain reference vs the "never hand-roll crypto" standard) and a couple of low-severity drifts. Fix the event-name vocabulary and the run_javascript reference before committing; the rest are quick cleanups.

findings.issue: Event-name vocabulary contradicts the canonical SSE schema. error-handling.md says errors surface 'through the canonical node:error and run:error events (see the SSE event schema)'. But reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md defines NO node:error or run:error — its canonical union is node:failed / run:failed (and run:cancelled). The cited canonical home directly contradicts the citing standard.
findings.proposedFix: Replace 'node:error and run:error' with the canonical 'node:failed and run:failed' (and reference run:cancelled where relevant). The error payload shape error:{code,message,retryable} matches node:failed exactly in the schema, so only the event names change.

findings.issue: Self-contradicting event vocabulary. The doc lists the 'colon-namespaced events' as including node:error and human_gate:pending, and the cost:updated narrative is fine, but node:error / human_gate:pending are NOT in the canonical SSE union (which has node:failed, human_gate:paused, human_gate:resumed, run:failed, run:cancelled). The same paragraph then declares the *dotted* names legacy/'wrong' and orders contributors to 'use the canonical names ... everywhere, including logs' — while the list it just gave is itself non-canonical. A reader cannot both obey this standard and match sse-event-schema.md.
findings.proposedFix: Rewrite the event list to exactly mirror sse-event-schema.md: run:started, node:started, agent:token, agent:tool_call, agent:tool_result, cost:updated, node:completed, node:failed, human_gate:paused, human_gate:resumed, run:completed, run:failed, run:cancelled. Keep the 'dotted names are legacy' rule; it is correct. The fix is to stop the standards from inventing a third (colon + error/pending) variant.

findings.issue: The unit-test list for packages/core asserts run events 'by their colon-namespaced names (node:started, ..., human_gate:pending, run:completed, run:error)'. human_gate:pending and run:error are not in the canonical SSE union (human_gate:paused/resumed, run:failed are). A test written to this standard would assert event names the engine never emits.
findings.proposedFix: Change human_gate:pending -> human_gate:paused (and add human_gate:resumed) and run:error -> run:failed to match sse-event-schema.md. Same one-line edit pattern as error-handling.md.

findings.issue: Hollow/dangling reference: the entire '## Sandbox for run_javascript' section, plus the 'when a review is mandatory' list, treat run_javascript as a shipped built-in tool and cite built-in-tools.md as 'the canonical tool contract'. But reference/shared-core/built-in-tools.md contains NO run_javascript — its catalog ships run_command (shell, allowlist-gated), http_request, search_web, etc. The only run_javascript mentions in the whole tree are this standard, the README summary, and the frozen _archive JSON. The standard pins a security control to a tool that does not exist in its own canonical home.
findings.proposedFix: Reconcile to one vocabulary. Either (a) rename the section to the actual tool — '## Sandbox / allowlist for run_command' — and describe the allowlist + FS-scope-tier model that built-in-tools.md actually documents (no ambient authority, allowlist required, scoped FS), or (b) if a JS-eval sandbox is genuinely intended (transform/condition nodes evaluate JS expressions per node-types.md), add run_javascript to built-in-tools.md as its canonical home first, then keep the standard. Do not leave the standard citing a tool absent from the catalog it links to. Also update standards/README.md and standards/code-review.md, which repeat 'run_javascript sandbox'.

findings.issue: Cross-doc conflict with the keychain reference on the 'never hand-roll crypto' rule. security-review.md mandates using 'vetted platform implementations (... platform AES-256-GCM ...) wrapped tightly' and 'A PR that rolls its own crypto is rejected'. But reference/desktop/keychain-and-secrets.md (Layer 2 file fallback) derives the AES-256-GCM key as 'a machine-specific secret XOR'd with a user-set master passphrase' — a hand-rolled, non-standard KDF (not PBKDF2/scrypt/Argon2), and XOR of a discoverable machine-id with a passphrase is exactly the kind of bespoke crypto construction the standard forbids. The standard and the reference it implicitly governs disagree.
findings.proposedFix: Either tighten the reference to a vetted KDF (Argon2id/scrypt over the passphrase, with the machine secret as additional salt — not XOR) so it complies with the standard, or, if the XOR scheme is deliberate, have security-review.md explicitly carve out and justify the key-derivation construction and cite it. As written they contradict. (Primary fix belongs in the reference; flagged here because the standard is the binding rule the reference violates.)

findings.issue: Template/corpus drift in the Related link text. adr-template.md and documentation-style.md §7 model the Related line as '[ADR-XXXX](XXXX-related-slug.md)', i.e. link text reading 'ADR-XXXX'. Every actual ADR in decisions/ uses the bare slug as link text instead ('[0002-vite-react-tanstack-not-nextjs.md](0002-...md)'). A contributor copying the template literally would produce link text inconsistent with the entire existing corpus.
findings.proposedFix: Make the template match the corpus: show '- **Related**: [0002-related-slug.md](0002-related-slug.md), [tech-stack.md](../tech-stack.md)' as the example, or alternatively normalize the corpus to 'ADR-XXXX' link text. Pick one; the template should demonstrate the form the corpus actually uses.

findings.proposedFix: Pin one home. Given testing.md unit-tests the FallbackChain runner under packages/llm ('packages/llm — the seam types, the ToolNormalizer, the CostTracker pricing table, the FallbackChain runner ...'), align error-handling.md/README wording to say the runner lives in packages/llm but owns policy above the adapters, and drop the '/packages/core' hedge in ADR-0011/seam — or vice versa. The standards should not assert a package location that project-structure.md flatly contradicts.

crossDocConflicts: RUN-EVENT VOCABULARY (systemic): standards/error-handling.md, standards/logging-and-observability.md, standards/testing.md, standards/security-review.md (via node:error) and architecture/execution-model.md + architecture/state-management.md all use node:error / run:error / human_gate:pending. findings.issue: KNOWN ISSUE #1 (Loop/Subworkflow forward-reference gap) — CONFIRMED and now fully traced. Phase 3 commits the canvas to building LoopNode: Outcomes line 23 ('all nine custom node types'), scope line 59, workstream 3.E line 242-244 (lists `LoopNode` in 'the nine custom node components'), run-mode 3.F line 278 ('the `LoopNode` iteration badge'), and milestone 3.M-canvas line 478 ('all 9 node types'). But (a) Phase 0 schema 0.E line 194-195 declares the authored node-type discriminated union as exactly EIGHT types — `input, agent, human_gate, condition, transform, parallel, merge, output` — with NO `loop`; (b) Phase 1's node handler workstream 1.P (lines 448-464) implements handlers ONLY for condition/fan_out/fan_in/transform/input/output (agent in 1.O, human gate in 1.Q) and 1.M (line 396) expands only the `parallel` construct — there is NO `loop` handler and NO `loop` expansion anywhere in Phase 1; (c) `Subworkflow` appears NOWHERE in the entire roadmap (not Phase 0, 1, or 3) despite the engine enum carrying it. So the canvas would render a LoopNode with no authored YAML `type` (reference marks it *(reserved)*) and no engine to execute it, and Subworkflow is an orphan engine enum value. node-types.md (line 24, 27) already marks Loop YAML and Subworkflow canvas+YAML as *(reserved)*. SCOPE RECOMMENDATION: RESERVE both, do not build in the first increment. Loop and Subworkflow stay forward-compat-only: keep `loop`/`subworkflow` in the engine enum and reserved in the YAML union (so committed files are forward-compatible), but DEFER the LoopNode canvas component and the loop/subworkflow engine handlers to a post-M5 increment. Building an iterating LoopNode requires loop-back-edge cycle handling that directly contradicts Phase 1's 'cycle = hard error' rule (1.M line 393-394), so it is not a small add-on.
findings.proposedFix: Make Phase 3 build EIGHT canvas node components, not nine: drop `LoopNode` from the 3.E list (line 242-244), the 3.F iteration-badge bullet (line 278), and change Outcomes line 23, scope line 59, and milestone 3.M-canvas (line 478) from 'nine'/'all 9 node types' to 'eight currently-executable node types (Loop/Subworkflow reserved, not yet executable per node-types.md)'. Correspondingly change node-types.md line 33 from 'renders nine custom ReactFlow node components' to 'eight currently-built components (LoopNode is specified but reserved/not-yet-built; Subworkflow has no component).' Add an explicit one-line 'Reserved, not in the first increment' note to both Phase 0 (0.E) and Phase 1 (1.P) stating loop/subworkflow stay in the engine enum for forward-compat but are not parsed/executed in Product Phase 1. If instead the team wants Loop built, it must be added to Phase 0 schema, given a Phase 1 1.P handler, and reconciled with the 'cycle = hard error' rule — a much larger change.

findings.issue: KNOWN ISSUE #3 (milestone-ID collision) — CONFIRMED. Workstream `3.M` is 'Workflow YAML import/export + starter templates' (graph node line 111, heading line 434, and referenced in the dependency edges and in the Completed-by column of milestones 3.M-canvas line 478 → '3.E, 3.M'). The SAME token `3.M` is simultaneously the prefix for the in-phase milestone IDs `3.M-shell`, `3.M-manage`, `3.M-canvas`, `3.M-theater`, `3.M-history` (lines 476-480). So `3.M` is ambiguously both a workstream id and the milestone-namespace prefix; the milestone table cell '3.E, 3.M' is genuinely confusing (is it workstream 3.M or a truncated milestone?). No other phase has this collision (Phase 1 uses `1.m1/1.m2`, Phase 4 uses `4.M1..4.M5`, Phase 5 uses `5.M1..5.M7`).
findings.proposedFix: Renumber the in-phase milestones to the numeric form used by every other phase: `3.M1` (shell), `3.M2` (manage), `3.M3` (canvas), `3.M4` (theater), `3.M5` (history), leaving the YAML-import workstream as the sole `3.M`. Update the milestone table (lines 474-481) accordingly. This also aligns Phase 3 with the `N.Mk` convention in Phases 1/4/5.

findings.issue: KNOWN ISSUE #4 (gate-paused exit code not pinned) — CONFIRMED, plus a NAME drift. The integer is unpinned in BOTH docs that must agree: commands.md Exit-codes table (lines 104-108) lists only 0/1/2 and the note (line 110) says 'the exact value is not yet locked'; roadmap Phase 2 workstream 2.A (lines 114-117) instructs to 'lock the value here' and 'document it in commands.md' but gives NO integer, and 2.F/2.G/exit-criteria refer only to 'the gate-paused code'. So neither the reference nor the roadmap states a concrete number — the lock was never performed. SEPARATELY there is a terminology drift for the same concept across three docs: commands.md line 68 calls it the 'gate-pending code', Phase 2 (lines 116, 202-203, 253, 288, 443) calls it 'gate-paused', and the CI tutorial (run-a-workflow-in-ci.md line 94) calls it a 'pause-pending state'. RECOMMENDED VALUE: exit code 3 (the next free integer after 0/1/2; conventional for a third deterministic outcome and does not collide with shell's 126/127/128+n reserved range).
findings.proposedFix: Pin exit code = 3 for gate-paused. In commands.md: add a `| 3 | Run paused awaiting a human-gate decision (non-interactive mode); resume with `relavium gate` |` row to the exit-codes table and delete the 'not yet locked' caveat on line 110; fix line 68 to say 'the gate-paused code (3)'. In phase-2-cli.md line 116 replace 'lock the value here, since the reference leaves it open' with 'use the locked value 3'. In run-a-workflow-in-ci.md line 77-80 add '3 = run paused at a human gate' to the inline list and rename 'pause-pending state' (line 94) to 'gate-paused'. Standardize on the single term 'gate-paused' everywhere (retire 'gate-pending' and 'pause-pending').

findings.issue: KNOWN ISSUE #2 (node-type taxonomy) — node-types.md IS the declared reconciliation source and the type/component NAME reconciliation (parallel→fan_out/fan_in, human_gate→human_in_the_loop→HumanGateNode, the 8-YAML/9-canvas/11-engine split) is internally coherent. BUT the reconciliation does NOT extend to enum VALUES of two config fields, leaving an unreconciled three-vocabulary drift a reader weeks from now cannot resolve: (1) merge/aggregator strategy values diverge across all three layers and only `concat` is shared — authored YAML (workflow-yaml-spec.md line 215) `merge_strategy: concat|object_merge|first|custom`; canvas AggregatorNode (node-types.md line 55) `concat|json_array|best_of_n|custom_js`; engine fan_in_config (node-types.md line 101) `merge_keys|collect_array|first_wins`. (2) The human-gate timeout field is named AND valued three different ways: workflow-yaml-spec.md line 174 `timeout_action: reject|approve|escalate`; node-types.md line 102 engine `on_timeout: fail|continue_default|cancel`; Phase 1 1.Q (line 479) uses `timeout_action` with values `fail|continue_default|cancel` (mixing the YAML name with the engine values). node-types.md line 106 hand-waves 'friendlier field names ... map onto the engine config blocks' but provides no value-level mapping table, so e.g. authored `merge_strategy: object_merge` → engine `merge_keys` is undocumented.
findings.proposedFix: In node-types.md add a short 'Value mapping' subsection (or extend the existing per-type engine-config table) that maps the authored YAML enum values to the engine enum values for the two fields that diverge: merge strategy (`object_merge`→`merge_keys`, `first`→`first_wins`, `custom`→`custom_js`, and reconcile `json_array`/`collect_array` and the canvas-only `best_of_n`) and gate timeout (`timeout_action: reject|approve|escalate` ↔ `on_timeout: fail|continue_default|cancel`, which currently don't even cover the same outcomes — `escalate` has no engine value, `cancel` has no authored value). Then fix Phase 1 1.Q line 479 to use the correct authored-vs-engine names consistently rather than mixing `timeout_action` (YAML name) with `fail/continue_default/cancel` (engine values).

findings.issue: Trigger-taxonomy drift between the idea note and the canonical workflow-yaml-spec. The idea note line 61 says the trigger field 'already enumerates `manual`, `file_change`, and RESERVES `schedule` / `webhook`' — but workflow-yaml-spec.md (lines 55-63, the one canonical home) lists `webhook` and `schedule` as FULL first-class trigger types with concrete extra-field schemas (`webhook: {path, secret_env}`, `schedule: <cron_expression>`), not 'reserved' placeholders, and additionally lists a FIFTH type `mcp_call` that the idea note omits entirely. The note's framing ('reserves schedule/webhook') understates the spec and silently drops `mcp_call`. (Phase 2 scope line 65 says 'only `manual` (and the engine's `file_change`)' exist in Phase 1, also omitting `mcp_call` — `mcp_call` has no phase note in the spec, so its Phase-1/2 status is undefined.)
findings.proposedFix: In the idea note, change line 61-62 to match the spec: '...the existing `trigger` field, which already enumerates `manual`, `file_change`, `mcp_call`, `webhook`, and `schedule` (webhook/schedule are declarable but require an always-on listener to fire automatically — a Phase-2 capability).' Separately, in workflow-yaml-spec.md add a phase note for `mcp_call` (line 63) clarifying whether it fires in Phase 1, and align Phase 2 scope line 65 to acknowledge `mcp_call` so the Phase-1 trigger set is stated consistently in all three docs.

findings.issue: pnpm `--filter` package-name drift that would break copy-pasteable commands. local-dev-setup.md uses `pnpm --filter @relavium/desktop tauri dev` (line 76) and `pnpm --filter @relavium/vscode-extension build` (line 84), but Phase 4 line 131 uses the UNSCOPED `pnpm --filter vscode-extension build`. project-structure.md (lines 49-50) pins only the directory names (`apps/desktop`, `apps/vscode-extension`) and the Marketplace id (`relavium.relavium`); it never declares an `@relavium/desktop` or `@relavium/vscode-extension` npm `name`. The CLI is consistently the bare name `relavium` (project-structure.md line 51, local-dev-setup.md line 68). Since `--filter` matches the package `name`, a wrong scope silently matches zero packages — this is a real foot-gun in the day-one setup runbook, and the two docs disagree on whether the desktop/extension packages are scoped.
findings.proposedFix: Pick one canonical package-name convention and apply it everywhere. Given the CLI is unscoped (`relavium`) and the apps live under `apps/*`, the simplest fix is to make local-dev-setup.md use the same unscoped form as Phase 4: `pnpm --filter desktop tauri dev` and `pnpm --filter vscode-extension build` (matching `apps/<dir>` names). Alternatively, if the apps ARE intended to be scoped (`@relavium/desktop`, `@relavium/vscode-extension`), then fix Phase 4 line 131 to use the scope and record the package-name convention in project-structure.md so all three docs agree. Either way, document the chosen package `name` values in project-structure.md as the one home.
